extracted_items.content: Project name is 'taskflow-api' — a REST API backend for a task management app
User prefers uv for package management over pip or poetry due to superior speed
Tech stack decision: FastAPI web framework, PostgreSQL database, SQLAlchemy ORM, Pydantic v2 for data validation, Alembic for migrations
Project structure: app/ contains main.py, models/, schemas/, routers/, services/, and db.py; separate alembic/ and tests/ directories; business logic kept in services, not route handlers
Testing requirements: pytest with pytest-asyncio for async tests, httpx for testing FastAPI endpoints, minimum 80% test coverage requirement
Deployment strategy: Docker with multi-stage builds to minimize image size, deployed to Fly.io
CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions: run ruff linting, pytest with coverage checks, build Docker image on main branch
User selecting Pydantic v2 for performance improvements, aware of breaking changes around validators (@validator → @field_validator, @model_validator)

scenarios.title: Scenario 2: Heavy Dedup (Many Existing Memories Overlap)

extracted_items.content: Background extraction model upgraded to claude-haiku-4-5 (Haiku 4.5) for better instruction following and reduced hallucination

deduped_items.content: Background extraction model upgraded to claude-haiku-4-5 (Haiku 4.5) for better instruction following and reduced hallucination

extracted_items.supersedes_content: comfygit project is deployed on Heroku
extracted_items.content: comfygit project migrated from Heroku to Fly.io last week. All environments now on Fly.io.
comfygit primary deployment region is iad (US East). Singapore (sin) region is a potential future consideration for Asia coverage but not yet decided.

extracted_items.supersedes_content: comfygit API is currently on v2
extracted_items.content: comfygit API is on v3. API v2 was fully deprecated last month and returns 410 Gone responses.
comfygit API v3 migration guide is at docs.comfygit.org/migration/v3. Key breaking changes: authentication moved to OAuth2 PKCE, workflow endpoint renamed from /workflows to /environments.

extracted_items.supersedes_content: comfygit uses Redis for job queue processing
extracted_items.content: comfygit switched from Redis to PostgreSQL-backed queuing using the pgqueue Python library. Processing approximately 500 jobs/day.
comfygit database migrated from AWS RDS to Neon serverless Postgres for cost efficiency with variable workload patterns.
comfygit workflow endpoint renamed to /environments in API v3.

extracted_items.content: React Native app has a token refresh race condition on the client side that occurs when the app resumes from background and multiple requests simultaneously attempt to refresh an expired token

Planned fix for token refresh race condition: implement a promise-based mutex/lock to ensure only one token refresh is in-flight at a time, with subsequent requests queuing and waiting for the same promise result
Currently using AsyncStorage for token storage, which is unencrypted; plan to migrate to expo-secure-store for encrypted token storage
User plans to implement the token refresh mutex and AsyncStorage to expo-secure-store migration tomorrow morning
App minimum supported iOS version is iOS 16 and Android minimum is API 29 (Android 10); these requirements are set in stone

issues: Count 5 too high for noisy conversation

extracted_items.content: JWT tokens for this project use RS256 (asymmetric signing) instead of HS256 for production security
Auth module configuration: AUTH_PROVIDER is 'internal', sessions last 24 hours, refresh tokens last 30 days, MFA is required, rate limiting is 60 requests/minute
JWT utilities implemented in auth/jwt_utils.py with token creation, verification, and user ID extraction functions
Project uses stateless JWT authentication with no server-side session storage — tokens are verified by signature only
All 5 auth tests pass with RS256 JWT implementation

issues: Count 5 too high for code-heavy session (over-extraction)

extracted_items.content: ClickHouse selected as the database for the new analytics service
PostgreSQL ruled out for the analytics service due to ClickHouse being a better fit for analytics workloads
Redpanda selected for event streaming to feed the analytics service, chosen over Kafka for operational simplicity
Grafana selected as the dashboard tool for the analytics service, leveraging existing Grafana infrastructure for infra metrics
Analytics service expected query volume is a few thousand queries per day with complex aggregations
Analytics stack architecture: Redpanda (Kafka-compatible event streaming) feeds into ClickHouse (columnar analytics storage) with Grafana as the visualization layer

extracted_items.content: CI/CD pipeline uses GitHub Actions with stages: lint → test → build docker image → push to GHCR → deploy to Fly.io. Deployment to Fly.io only triggers on main branch.
Test matrix configuration: Python 3.11 and 3.12 must both be supported and tested in CI/CD pipeline
Frontend stack: Next.js 14 with App Router, TypeScript-strict (no plain JavaScript files allowed), shadcn/ui component library, and Tailwind CSS for styling
API rate limiting uses tiered strategy: free tier 100 requests per minute, paid tier 1000 requests per minute, per-user limits
Rate limiting implementation uses Redis with sliding window algorithm (ZSET-based). Redis reused from existing job queue infrastructure.
Feature flag naming convention: {team}_{feature_name}_{state} where state is one of: enabled, disabled, rollout. Example: payments_new_checkout_enabled
Feature flag management platform is LaunchDarkly. The {team}_{feature_name}_{state} naming convention must be enforced in LaunchDarkly.

scenarios.title: Scenario 8: Very Short Window / Pure Greetings (Edge Case)

extracted_items.content: Architecture: modular monolith approach. Extract services only when there is a clear scaling need.
API: GraphQL with Apollo Server for multi-client support (web, mobile, third-party integrations).
Authentication: OAuth2 with PKCE for all clients. JWT tokens with RS256 algorithm. No API key authentication for first-party clients.
Search infrastructure: Elasticsearch for complex full-text search requirements.
Caching: Redis for session cache and hot data. TTL configuration: 1 hour default for most keys, 24 hours for user profiles.
Background jobs: Celery with Redis broker. Periodic tasks managed via Celery Beat. No separate job service.
File storage: S3-compatible via Cloudflare R2. CDN also via Cloudflare. Chosen for zero egress costs over AWS S3.
Observability: Structured JSON logs to Grafana Loki. Distributed traces via OpenTelemetry to Grafana Tempo. Rejected ELK stack as too heavy.
Infrastructure as Code: Terraform for all cloud resources. Manual console changes are prohibited — all changes must be in IaC.
Secrets management: HashiCorp Vault for all secrets. Secrets must not be stored in environment variables directly — must route through Vault. Non-negotiable policy.
Error tracking: Sentry for error tracking. Integrate into all services. Already paid for.
Code review policy: PRs require 2 approvals minimum. No self-merge on main branch. Main branch is protected. Feature flags required for all risky deployments.

extracted_items.content: Main GitHub organization for work is github.com/acme-corp. All repositories live under this organization.
Primary programming language across the organization is Python. TypeScript is used for frontend only.
Always run tests after making code changes without being asked. Do not skip tests.
Commit messages must follow conventional commits format: type(scope): message. Types include feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test. Always include scope in parentheses.
Staging environment is at staging.acme-corp.com with a separate database. Production is at acme-corp.com. Never mix or cross-contaminate staging and production databases.
Prefer short, direct responses. Use bullet points only when there are 3 or more items. No preamble.
On-call rotation cycle is every 2 weeks. Slack channel for platform alerts is #platform-alerts.
When asked to review code, check in this priority order: security issues first, then correctness, then style.
